item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My god, I love malware related skips, and you did one with such an interesting effect? I love how you focused more on the virus than on the symptoms. The email seems slightly out of place though, but I enjoyed the mystery it caused. Overall amazing skip I will probably go back to every so often.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a competently written but ultimately boring execution of the "What if Computer Viruses were Real Viruses?" idea. Cute, but there's nothing here that I'd upvote for.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Personnel at any site exposed to SCP-1900 or reporting lesions, blisters, headaches, or seizures are to be examined and, if found to be infected, quarantined indefinitely, and all internet-capable devices they own destroyed.

Is that supposed to say that personnel at that site who report lesions, etc should be examined (which makes sense), or that anyone at any site who has "…headaches…" should be examined? Which is what it actually say, and holy f*ck does that sound like a waste of resources — treating every headache among people with a high stress job as a potential infection?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Some people have been adapting SCPs for scenarios in Plague Inc. Evolved. This would be a remarkably sexy candidate.

Request for additional data regarding transmission: Are both the activated and latent stages in humans contagious? (Thankfully, I'm not familiar with herpes.) Is computer-to-human transmission possible at all times post-infection, or just when the attachment is opened?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There is something about the e-mail, and the typos in it, that made me upvote. It's quite similar to the ordinary spam mails that are all around and that gives the SCP plausibility. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You mean pseudo-Greek, not pseudo-Latin. The suffix is like Latin but the roots are Greek.

The only other question I could ask is "Since when would you find a physics book at Wal-Mart?!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really well written, not my cup of tea though topic wise, kudos on the other hand. And I'm assuming you mean 'low-viability' as in its hard to find people with it? Like how a cold has lots of outside physical feature like runny nose, sneezing ect. I don't know, just want to clarify.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Right on Androphilus, Bryshevskiy was just a portmanteau of the last names of two Ukranian guys I know. That's how I get most names.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I assumed Androphilus was a pseudo-latin reference. From the context of the e-mail, I would loosely translate it as "lover of mankind".
Don't know enough Russian to speculat, but wal-mart has a physics book by a very similarly named author.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
G2 is a rarely used designation for sites that contain highly dangerous biohazard materials. SCP-008 imply that G2 Sites weren't build by the Foundation, but by some unknown party. Site-15 is a storage facility for electronic SCP objects, and was almost certainly constructed by the Foundation. How and what is G2 Site 15 I don't know and don't much care, since there is no canon and the rest of the article is really good.

I agree that low-visibility don't quite fit in that sentence.

I wonder if I should recognize names Bryshevskiy and Androphilus. Is this a shot-out to something, or were they just made up on the spot?

EDIT: This is a computer-related SCP object that don't make me want to slam my head on the wall repeatedly. That's quite rare. Also, the sentence "Additionally, imaging reveals abnormal brainwave patterns consistent and synchronized across all patients." is just marvelous. This is one of those occasions where I want to go and upvote the article a second time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While small parts are similar, I'd say you succeeded. I already upvoted. Sorry for any confusion.

+ Completely unrelated trivia/side note
- bonus edition

SCP-1900 was the only Herpes released that day, the other one was Chicken Pox.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was trying to avoid being too similar to Snow Crash. Hopefully I succeeded on that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If you consider an itchy rash fun….

Anyway, all the fun of the virus from Snowcrash without all the memetic baggage attached, with a whiff of future "human botnet" added for flavor.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's called jacking off.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now I can experience all the fun of sex without leaving my computer!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
G2 Site 15

Is G2 a room at Site 15? An area within Site 15?

As SCP-1900 is already widespread among the civilian population, low-visibility,

I think instead of "low-visibility" you mean "hard to detect", or something similar.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just the fact that this is a virus both biological and technological was enough to get my upvote. The containment procedures are some of the most interesting I've ever read.

item.title: Re: See?
item.content:encoded:
What did I tell you?
Just sandbox it, and it would work out!
I love it!
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Goddamit! I was working on a computer-virus SCP! :(
Ah, well, you did it better than I ever could…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
After that awfully-written SCPs streak it is nice to read a really good one.

You, sir, have my +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a pretty original idea, and it's well executed. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very nice use of the herpes virus. Well-written. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Herpes tends to do that

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This one tickles me in all my love spots. +1
